flashcards.q: What is the axis of rotation of the Earth?
flashcards.a: An imaginary line passing through the geographic North Pole and South Pole, around which the Earth rotates.

flashcards.q: Does the Earth rotate clockwise or anti-clockwise when viewed from above the North Pole?
flashcards.a: Anti-clockwise (West to East).

flashcards.q: Due to Earth's rotation, in which direction does the Sun appear to rise and set?
flashcards.a: The Sun appears to rise in the East and set in the West.

flashcards.q: How long does the Earth take to complete one revolution around the Sun?
flashcards.a: Approximately 365 days and 6 hours (about 1 year).

flashcards.q: What is the shape of the Earth's orbit around the Sun when viewed from the top?
flashcards.a: Nearly circular.

flashcards.q: True or False: The Earth's axis of rotation is perfectly upright with respect to its orbit.
flashcards.a: False. The Earth's axis is tilted with respect to the plane of its orbit.

flashcards.q: In which month is the Earth closest to the Sun?
flashcards.a: January.

flashcards.q: What season does the Northern Hemisphere experience in June?
flashcards.a: Summer — because the Northern Hemisphere is tilted towards the Sun.

flashcards.q: What season does the Southern Hemisphere experience in June?
flashcards.a: Winter — because the Southern Hemisphere is tilted away from the Sun in June.

flashcards.q: What is the winter solstice date in the Northern Hemisphere?
flashcards.a: Around 22 December — the shortest day and longest night.

flashcards.q: On which two dates do spring and autumn equinoxes occur in the Northern Hemisphere?
flashcards.a: Spring Equinox: ~21 March; Autumn Equinox: ~23 September.

flashcards.q: How many hours of day and night does the equator receive throughout the year?
flashcards.a: Always approximately 12 hours of day and 12 hours of night, throughout the year.

flashcards.q: How many hours of continuous sunlight does the North Pole receive after 21 March?
flashcards.a: Continuous sunlight (24 hours/day) for six months — from the spring equinox (~21 March) to the autumn equinox (~22 September).

flashcards.q: What happens at the South Pole when the North Pole has six months of sunlight?
flashcards.a: The South Pole experiences six months of continuous darkness (and vice versa).

flashcards.q: Why do the stars also appear to rise in the East and set in the West?
flashcards.a: Because the Earth rotates from West to East — just like the Sun, all stars appear to move in the opposite direction (East to West).

flashcards.q: Why does the Moon also appear to rise in the East and set in the West?
flashcards.a: Because the Earth rotates from West to East, making the Moon (like the Sun and stars) appear to move from East to West.

flashcards.q: Which star appears nearly stationary in the night sky and why?
flashcards.a: The Pole Star (Dhruva Tara) — because the Earth's axis of rotation points very close to it, so it barely shifts position as the Earth rotates.

flashcards.q: What are star trails?
flashcards.a: Arcs traced by stars in long-exposure photographs, caused by the apparent motion of stars due to Earth's rotation.

flashcards.q: Who invented the Foucault pendulum and what does it prove?
flashcards.a: Léon Foucault (19th century). It proves the Earth's rotation — the pendulum's swing plane appears to rotate as the Earth turns beneath it.

flashcards.q: Where is a Foucault pendulum installed in India?
flashcards.a: In the Constitution Hall of the new Parliament building in New Delhi (22 metres long).

flashcards.q: What is the name of Aryabhata's treatise in which he explained the apparent motion of stars?
flashcards.a: Aryabhatiya (Verse 9, Golapada).

flashcards.q: What value did Aryabhata calculate for the Earth's rotation period?
flashcards.a: Approximately 23 hours 56 minutes 4.1 seconds — remarkably close to the currently accepted value.

flashcards.q: Which community used star patterns to predict the arrival of monsoon rains?
flashcards.a: The Bhil and Pawara communities from the Tapi Valley in western India.

flashcards.q: What is apparent size?
flashcards.a: How large an object appears to an observer, which depends on both the object's actual size and its distance from the observer.

flashcards.q: What is the arrangement of the Sun, Moon, and Earth during a solar eclipse?
flashcards.a: Sun → Moon → Earth (the Moon is between the Sun and the Earth).

flashcards.q: What is the arrangement of the Sun, Earth, and Moon during a lunar eclipse?
flashcards.a: Sun → Earth → Moon (the Earth is between the Sun and the Moon).

flashcards.q: On what phase of the Moon does a solar eclipse occur?
flashcards.a: New Moon (Amavasya).

flashcards.q: On what phase of the Moon does a lunar eclipse occur?
flashcards.a: Full Moon (Purnima).

flashcards.q: What is the 'diamond ring' effect seen during a solar eclipse?
flashcards.a: It is the bright flash of sunlight seen just as the Moon begins to move away after a total solar eclipse, resembling a diamond ring.

flashcards.q: Why do Mercury and Venus appear as tiny dots and cannot cover the Sun?
flashcards.a: Although larger than the Moon in actual size, they are much farther from Earth — so their apparent sizes are very small compared to the Sun.

flashcards.q: What is a Transit of Venus?
flashcards.a: A rare event when Venus passes between the Sun and the Earth, appearing as a tiny black dot moving across the Sun's bright face.

flashcards.q: What colour does the Moon appear during a total lunar eclipse?
flashcards.a: Dark red (sometimes called a 'blood moon').

flashcards.q: What causes the Moon to appear dark red during a total lunar eclipse?
flashcards.a: Earth's atmosphere refracts (bends) red wavelengths of sunlight around Earth's curvature, directing them onto the Moon.

flashcards.q: Name three safe ways to observe a solar eclipse.
flashcards.a: Certified solar eclipse goggles; projecting the Sun's image using a mirror onto a wall; attending a planetarium or astronomy club eclipse viewing event.

flashcards.q: What is an eclipse called in Sanskrit?
flashcards.a: Grahan.

flashcards.q: Which ancient Indian text contains calculations to predict eclipses?
flashcards.a: Surya Siddhanta.

flashcards.q: Where is the Kodaikanal Solar Observatory located and when was it established?
flashcards.a: In the Palani hills of southern India (Tamil Nadu). Established in 1899; operated by the Indian Institute of Astrophysics (IIA), Bengaluru.

flashcards.q: Who is known as the father of modern Indian astronomy?
flashcards.a: M. K. Vainu Bappu.

flashcards.q: After which scientist is the observatory at Kavalur, Tamil Nadu named?
flashcards.a: M. K. Vainu Bappu.

flashcards.q: Why do sunrises occur earlier in eastern India than in western India?
flashcards.a: Because the Earth rotates from West to East, eastern locations rotate into sunlight before western locations.

flashcards.q: Why does Earth's revolution cause the night sky constellations to change over the year?
flashcards.a: As Earth orbits the Sun, it faces different regions of space each night — so different stars and constellations become visible in different months.

flashcards.q: True or False: Seasons occur because the Earth is closer to the Sun in summer.
flashcards.a: False. Seasons are caused by the tilt of Earth's axis and Earth's spherical shape, not by Earth's distance from the Sun.

flashcards.q: What two effects of axial tilt combine to cause a hemisphere's summer?
flashcards.a: (1) Sunrays strike at a steeper angle — more concentrated (intense) heat. (2) Daytime lasts more than 12 hours — longer exposure to sunlight.

flashcards.q: Why is the effect of seasons minimal near the equator?
flashcards.a: Near the equator, the Sun's angle and the duration of daylight change very little throughout the year, so temperature stays relatively constant.

flashcards.q: True or False: Every New Moon causes a solar eclipse and every Full Moon causes a lunar eclipse.
flashcards.a: False. Eclipses only occur when the Moon is near the points where its orbit crosses Earth's orbital plane — not every New Moon or Full Moon.
